schema.unlock.type: free | level | class_level | discovery | chapter_reward | milestone
schema.unlock.level: Required for 'level' and 'class_level' — user level needed
schema.unlock.class: Required for 'class_level' — restricts entry to one class
schema.unlock.milestone_id: Required for 'milestone' — template_id of the milestone
schema.unlock.chapter_id: Required for 'chapter_reward' — chapter that rewards this entry
schema.rarity: null | common | rare | legendary (Memory fragments only)
schema.discovery_config: Probabilities used by the CLI when randomly discovering Memory fragments

entries.title: The Six Great Orders
entries.content:
Though all followers of Questline seek progress, few agree on how it should be achieved.

Over the centuries, six distinct philosophies emerged.

Each became an Order.

Each mastered a different aspect of productivity.

Each insists they are obviously correct.

The resulting arguments have lasted for generations.

entries.title: The Council of Orders
entries.content:
Though the Orders often disagree, they meet each year at the Hall of Progress.

Representatives gather to discuss threats facing the realm.

The Great Backlog.

Scope Dragons.

Deadline Wraiths.

Meeting Mimics.

Notification Sprites.

And other horrors.

The meetings usually begin with noble intentions.

They usually end with action items.

The action items are recorded.

Assigned.

Prioritized.

Scheduled.

Categorized.

Linked to supporting documentation.

And occasionally completed.

entries.title: The Terminal Covenant
entries.content:
The Code Warlocks

"It worked on my machine."

No one knows exactly how the Code Warlocks began.

Their own records are incomplete.

Mostly because they forgot to back them up.

According to legend, the first Code Warlock discovered an ancient terminal hidden beneath the ruins of a forgotten data center.

Within it were the Sacred Commands.

Many were dangerous.

Several were undocumented.

One simply read:

sudo trust_me

History records that this was a mistake.

entries.title: The Great Forking
entries.content:
The most famous event in Warlock history was The Great Forking.

A disagreement regarding indentation escalated into a civil war.

Entire repositories split apart.

Friendships ended.

Three documentation teams disappeared.

To this day no one remembers the original argument.

Only that it was important.

entries.title: Traditions
entries.content:
Code Warlocks consume sacred caffeinated beverages before performing major rituals.

The stronger the coffee, the more powerful the magic.

This belief remains scientifically unchallenged.

entries.title: Rivalries
entries.content:
Code Warlocks and Systems Architects have argued for centuries.

Warlocks believe systems should emerge naturally.

Architects believe systems should be designed beforehand.

The resulting meetings are responsible for approximately 14% of all productivity losses in recorded history.

entries.title: The Sacred Checklist
entries.content:
The Task Paladins

"Finish what you start."

The Task Paladins are the defenders of execution.

While others debate.

While others plan.

While others research.

Task Paladins complete things.

They maintain that motivation is unreliable.

Discipline is dependable.

And checking a box feels incredible.

entries.title: The Endless List
entries.content:
At the center of their Order lies a stone tablet known as The Endless List.

Every unfinished task in existence is said to appear upon its surface.

Fortunately, the tablet is several kilometers tall.

Otherwise morale would suffer considerably.

entries.title: Traditions
entries.content:
Young Paladins swear the Oath of Completion.

The oath is simple:

"I will stop creating new projects before finishing old ones."

Very few survive their first year.

entries.title: Rivalries
entries.content:
Task Paladins view Scope Dragons as their natural enemies.

Unfortunately, Scope Dragons often disguise themselves as exciting opportunities.

This has resulted in numerous tragic incidents.

entries.title: The Silent Archive
entries.content:
The Mind Sages

"That reminds me of a note I took six years ago."

The Mind Sages dedicate themselves to preserving knowledge.

Nothing is too small to record.

Nothing is too obscure to catalog.

Nothing is too ridiculous to link to three related concepts.

Their archives contain billions of interconnected ideas.

Many visitors become permanently lost.

Fortunately, the Sages have detailed maps explaining how to escape.

Unfortunately, those maps require reading seventeen prerequisite notes.

entries.title: The Great Linking
entries.content:
A legendary Sage once connected every note in the Archive to every other note.

The resulting structure became so complex that it achieved sentience.

The Archive still occasionally recommends books no one remembers writing.

entries.title: Traditions
entries.content:
Initiates are given a single blank page.

Their task is simple:

Write something worth remembering.

Most spend years preparing.

Some never begin.

A few immediately write:

"Don't overthink this."

These individuals are usually promoted.

entries.title: Rivalries
entries.content:
Mind Sages secretly believe everyone else's systems would improve if they simply took better notes.

Everyone else secretly fears they may be right.

entries.title: The Builders of Order
entries.content:
The Systems Architects

"Let's step back and look at the bigger picture."

No phrase has ever inspired more hope and fear simultaneously.

Systems Architects see patterns where others see chaos.

Processes where others see confusion.

Structure where others see piles of unrelated documents.

They possess an almost supernatural ability to create organization.

Many are capable of producing folder hierarchies before understanding the project itself.

entries.title: The Great Refactoring
entries.content:
One Architect famously reorganized an entire kingdom.

Roads were rerouted.

Guilds were restructured.

Departments were merged.

Everything became dramatically more efficient.

No one could find anything for six months.

entries.title: Traditions
entries.content:
Architects spend years studying the Sacred Frameworks.

Every generation eventually invents a new framework.

Every generation claims it solves all previous problems.

History suggests otherwise.

entries.title: Rivalries
entries.content:
Architects often clash with Task Paladins.

Paladins want action.

Architects want planning.

Together they accidentally create functional organizations.

entries.title: The Keepers of Hours
entries.content:
The Time Chronomancers

"That meeting could have been an email."

The Time Chronomancers study the most precious resource in existence:

Time.

Unlike gold, time cannot be earned.

Unlike knowledge, time cannot be stored.

Unlike tasks, time refuses to wait.

Chronomancers dedicate their lives to understanding where it goes.

Most discoveries are deeply unsettling.

entries.title: The Lost Afternoon
entries.content:
Among their greatest mysteries is The Lost Afternoon.

A temporal anomaly affecting productivity across the realm.

Victims sit down for five minutes.

Three hours vanish.

No explanation has ever been found.

Researchers suspect social media.

entries.title: Traditions
entries.content:
Chronomancer apprentices carry hourglasses at all times.

Not because they are useful.

Because it looks extremely impressive.

entries.title: Rivalries
entries.content:
Chronomancers frequently argue with Mind Sages.

Chronomancers believe notes should be brief.

Mind Sages believe brevity is reckless.

These debates often last several hours.

Which greatly annoys the Chronomancers.

entries.title: The Order of the Ledger
entries.content:
The Arch Accountants

"If it is not recorded, it did not happen."

The Arch Accountants were among the first followers of the Questline.

Where others sought glory, they sought balance.

Where others chased inspiration, they chased documentation.

Where others asked "Can we afford this?"

The Arch Accountants replied:

"We could have answered that three months ago if someone had updated the spreadsheet."

entries.title: Business Purposes
entries.content:
Their temples are vast halls of ledgers, records, receipts, reports, and financial histories stretching back centuries.

Every transaction is preserved.

Every expense is categorized.

Every discrepancy is investigated.

Especially the suspicious charge labeled:

"Business Purposes."

No one has ever successfully explained a Business Purposes expense to an Arch Accountant.

entries.title: Traditions
entries.content:
New initiates must perform the Rite of Reconciliation.

A sacred ceremony in which a financial statement refuses to balance by exactly $0.03.

The ritual continues until the discrepancy is found.

Some initiates emerge wiser.

Others emerge with eye twitches.

entries.title: Rivalries
entries.content:
Arch Accountants maintain a long-standing rivalry with Code Warlocks.

The Accountants claim developers spend money recklessly.

The Warlocks claim budgets are imaginary.

Both sides are technically correct.

entries.title: Before the First Cursor
entries.content:
Before there were projects, before there were tasks, before there were notes and chronicles, there was only the Void.

The Void was not empty.

It was filled with unfinished intentions.

Ideas that would someday be started.

Plans that would someday be organized.

Goals that would someday be pursued.

Yet none of them ever moved beyond possibility.

Nothing was recorded.

Nothing was completed.

Nothing endured.

This forgotten era became known as The Age of Intention.

entries.title: The Age of Open Tabs
entries.content:
As civilization advanced, the people attempted to impose order upon their lives.

They created notes.

They created lists.

They created plans.

Unfortunately, they created them everywhere.

Entire kingdoms buried themselves beneath scattered notebooks, forgotten documents, abandoned projects, and browser windows numbering in the hundreds.

Scholars estimate that some individuals maintained over seventy active tabs simultaneously.

Few survived.

Historians still debate whether productivity truly existed during this period.

entries.title: The Rise of the Great Backlog
entries.content:
Every unfinished task leaves behind a trace.

A postponed promise.

An ignored responsibility.

A project that would be completed "later."

For centuries these fragments accumulated.

Eventually they combined into something terrible.

The Great Backlog.

No one knows its true form.

Some describe a mountain.

Others a storm.

Still others claim it resembles an email inbox with twenty-seven thousand unread messages.

Whatever its nature, its influence spread throughout the world.

Projects stalled.

Deadlines collapsed.

Entire organizations vanished beneath the weight of unfinished work.

entries.title: The First Cursor
entries.content:
During the darkest days of the Great Backlog, a lone traveler discovered a blinking light within the Void.

It appeared as a single cursor.

Patient.

Silent.

Waiting.

The traveler approached.

The cursor blinked.

The traveler blinked.

Neither moved for some time.

Eventually the traveler spoke the words:

"Hello World."

Light spread through the darkness.

Structure emerged.

Projects took form.

Notes gained permanence.

Tasks acquired purpose.

The First Cursor had awakened.

entries.title: The Founding of Questline
entries.content:
In the years that followed, the surviving peoples gathered around the teachings of the First Cursor.

They learned that progress was not achieved through motivation.

Progress was achieved through repetition.

Through systems.

Through consistency.

Through showing up again tomorrow.

These teachings became known as the Questline.

Not because the path was easy.

But because every meaningful achievement was composed of smaller quests completed one after another.

entries.title: The Six Great Orders
entries.content:
As Questline spread across the realm, different groups interpreted its teachings in different ways.

Some pursued structure.

Others sought discipline.

Some mastered knowledge.

Others mastered time itself.

From these philosophies emerged the Six Great Orders.

The Arch Accountants.

The Code Warlocks.

The Mind Sages.

The Task Paladins.

The Systems Architects.

The Time Chronomancers.

Though their methods differed, all sought the same goal:

To bring order to chaos and purpose to effort.

entries.title: The Era of Productivity
entries.content:
For the first time in history, progress became measurable.

Projects were completed.

Goals were achieved.

Knowledge was preserved.

Entire cities prospered under the guidance of the Orders.

Yet success created new challenges.

Scope Dragons multiplied.

Meeting Mimics infiltrated institutions.

Deadline Wraiths appeared in increasing numbers.

The struggle against chaos had entered a new age.

entries.title: The Growth of The Evergrowth
entries.content:
During this period, a mysterious sapling appeared near the center of the realm.

No one knows who planted it.

No one knows where it came from.

Attempts to accelerate its growth failed.

Attempts to manipulate it failed.

Attempts to place it in a productivity framework generated seventeen conflicting methodologies and three conference talks.

The Tree ignored them all.

It grew only through consistent effort.

A little each day.

Never quickly.

Never dramatically.

Yet never stopping.

entries.title: The Age of Chronicles
entries.content:
The greatest weakness of mortals had always been memory.

Victories were forgotten.

Progress went unnoticed.

Growth became invisible.

To solve this, the Orders created the Chronicle.

A living record of journeys, achievements, failures, discoveries, and lessons learned.

The Chronicle does not celebrate perfection.

It celebrates persistence.

Every completed task.

Every finished project.

Every return after a difficult day.

All are recorded.

entries.title: The Present Age
entries.content:
The realm now stands in an era unlike any before it.

The Great Backlog remains beyond the horizon.

Deadline Wraiths continue to roam.

Scope Dragons still tempt adventurers with promises of "just one more feature."

Yet the people endure.

Every day new travelers begin their journey.

Every day new quests are completed.

Every day another page is added to the Chronicle.

The story of Questline remains unfinished.

As all good stories should.

entries.title: The Fate of the Notification Sprites
entries.content:
When the Swarm finally broke, no great battle was recorded.

No armies marched.

No ancient relic was activated.

No chosen hero stood atop a mountain and delivered a dramatic speech.

The Sprites simply began to vanish.

One by one.

Then hundreds at a time.

Then thousands.

Across the Realm, unfinished quests were completed.

Scrolls were written.

Reflections were recorded.

Focus sessions were honored.

The Swarm had always fed upon hesitation.

Every ignored task.

Every postponed intention.

Every promise made to "start tomorrow."

The Notification Sprites themselves were never evil.

Merely hungry.

For years they had consumed abandoned attention and multiplied beyond control.

But once the Realm began moving forward again, the food supply disappeared.

The Sprites weakened.

Their numbers collapsed.

Many returned to their natural role as harmless messengers.

Others scattered into forgotten corners of the Realm.

Yet as the final Swarm dissolved, the Mind Sages noticed something troubling.

The Sprites had never created the problem.

They had only benefited from it.

Something else had nurtured the conditions that allowed the Swarm to grow.

Something patient.

Something ancient.

Something that preferred heroes distracted.

At the edge of recorded history, the Chronicle found references to a force long believed dormant.

A force known only as:

The Great Backlog.

The horizon darkened.

The Realm celebrated its victory.

But the Chronicle quietly turned to the next page.

entries.title: Reluctant Hero
entries.content:
Tier I — Initiate Milestone: First Quest

You completed a task, wrote a note, and acknowledged the project existed for at least one day.

The bar was on the floor.

You found it.

"Every legend begins somewhere. Some begin more enthusiastically than others."

— Questline Archives

entries.title: Amateur Historian
entries.content:
Tier I — Initiate Milestone: Chronicle Keeper

You showed up on two different days and wrote about it.

Future archaeologists will be politely unimpressed.

History is indeed written by those who show up — just not always read by anyone.

"The chronicle does not judge. It simply records. Unlike historians, who absolutely judge."

— Questline Archives

entries.title: Accidental Monk
entries.content:
Tier I — Initiate Milestone: Focused Adventurer

Three focus sessions without rage-quitting.

Your attention span has outlasted most relationships and several national governments.

This was not the plan. It worked anyway.

"Silence is the loudest productivity tool. You have now heard it three times."

— Questline Archives

entries.title: Management Material
entries.content:
Tier II — Veteran Milestone: Realm Builder

Ten tasks completed. Seven days of project age. Five days of actual presence.

You have officially done more structured follow-through than most people accomplish in a calendar quarter.

This is now a personality trait.

Accept it.

"A realm worth building takes time. You have now proven this through evidence."

— Questline Archives

entries.title: Unnecessary Biographer
entries.content:
Tier II — Veteran Milestone: Keeper of the Chronicle

Fifteen tasks. Five journal entries. Seven active days over at least a week.

You have documented a project that probably no one asked about with the dedication of someone who absolutely did not need to ask.

Thorough. Possibly alarming.

"The chronicle awaits further deeds. It has been waiting a while. It is patient."

— Questline Archives

unlock.display: Earn the Keeper of the Chronicle milestone

entries.title: Creature of Habit
entries.content:
Tier II — Veteran Milestone: Steady Hero

A seven-day streak. Twenty completed tasks. Ten daily adventures.

You are either genuinely productive or you have confused discipline for a coping mechanism.

Both explanations are statistically consistent with the evidence.

"Heroes are forged through daily discipline. You have now been forged. Try not to rust."

— Questline Archives

entries.title: Probably Fine
entries.content:
Tier III — Legendary Milestone: Master of Realms

Fifty tasks. Twenty notes. Twenty active days. Thirty days of project age.

You have built something large enough that you can no longer remember where it started.

The paperwork is, however, immaculate.

Carry on.

"Mastery is the sum of ten thousand small victories. You have achieved at least fifty of them."

— Questline Archives

unlock.display: Earn the Master of Realms milestone

entries.title: Unsolicited Archivist
entries.content:
Tier III — Legendary Milestone: Legend of the Chronicle

One hundred tasks. Twenty-five journal entries. Thirty active days on a project at least thirty days old.

You have documented your productivity so thoroughly that your documentation now has its own subtext.

Future scholars will cite you.

They will not know why.

"Your story is now legend. Whether anyone reads it is a separate question entirely."

— Questline Archives

unlock.display: Earn the Legend of the Chronicle milestone

entries.title: The Myth. The Legend. The Problem.
entries.content:
Tier III — Legendary Milestone: Avatar of Completion

One hundred tasks. Twenty-five daily adventures. A thirty-day streak.

This is either exceptional discipline or a warning sign.

Either way, the backlog has stopped trying to intimidate you.

It now simply respects you from a cautious distance.

"You have become the hero the world needed. The world did not ask for this. It is grateful anyway."

— Questline Archives

unlock.display: Earn the Avatar of Completion milestone

entries.title: Fragment #001 — Sir Aldric the Hesitant
entries.content:
Recovered from the Third Age of Checklists

"I kept postponing the task because I wanted to do it perfectly.

Three months later I realized doing it badly would have been sufficient."

— Sir Aldric the Hesitant
Task Paladin, Level 63

Status: Eventually completed.

entries.title: Fragment #002 — Warlock Bryn of Forty-Seven Tabs
entries.content:
Recovered from a damaged project archive

"Today I spent six hours building a system to save five minutes.

Tomorrow I shall spend another six improving it."

— Warlock Bryn of Forty-Seven Tabs

Status: Project still active.

entries.title: Fragment #003 — Accountant General Harlan
entries.content:
Recovered from the Great Backlog War

"The dragon was terrifying.

The budget meeting was worse."

— Accountant General Harlan

Status: Unknown.

entries.title: Fragment #004 — Systems Architect Vexa
entries.content:
Recovered from an abandoned notebook

"If you have reorganized the same folder four times,
you are no longer organizing.

You are decorating."

— Systems Architect Vexa

Status: Folder renamed twice more.

entries.title: Fragment #005 — Chronomancer Elian
entries.content:
Recovered from the Hall of Clocks

"The deadline was visible from the beginning.

I simply believed I was special."

— Chronomancer Elian

Status: Deadline victorious.

entries.title: Fragment #006 — Unknown Hero
entries.content:
Recovered from a damaged coffee-stained scroll

"The task took twenty minutes.

Avoiding the task took three weeks."

— Unknown Hero

Status: Classic.

entries.title: Fragment #007 — Sage Corvin
entries.content:
Recovered from the Archives of the Mind Sages

"I took excellent notes.

Unfortunately they were spread across seven notebooks,
three applications,
and one napkin."

— Sage Corvin

Status: Information technically preserved.

entries.title: Fragment #008 — Chronicle Entry 11,204
entries.content:
Recovered from the Chronicle

"The hero requested motivation.

The Chronicle provided a deadline."

— Chronicle Entry 11,204

entries.title: Fragment #009 — Fellowship Log #382
entries.content:
Recovered from an ancient project board

"Halfway through the project,
we realized nobody remembered why we started."

— Fellowship Log #382

entries.title: Fragment #010 — The Last Finisher
entries.content:
Recovered from a sealed vault

"The greatest productivity technique I ever discovered
was beginning."

— The Last Finisher

Status: Confirmed.

entries.title: Fragment #077 — The Sixth Chronicle
entries.content:
Recovered from a forbidden archive

"The Great Backlog can never be destroyed.

Only managed."

— The Sixth Chronicle

[ Rare Fragment ]

entries.title: Fragment #112 — Rootkeeper Sol
entries.content:
Recovered from the roots of The Evergrowth

"Heroes ask how long the tree takes to grow.

The tree asks how long they plan to remain."

— Rootkeeper Sol

[ Rare Fragment ]

entries.title: Fragment #144 — Future You
entries.content:
Recovered from the Future

"Everything worked out.

Now stop worrying and finish the task."

— Future You

[ Rare Fragment ]

entries.title: Fragment #188 — Chronomancer Voss
entries.content:
Recovered from a corrupted timeline

"I finally reached Inbox Zero.

Nobody was there to witness it."

— Chronomancer Voss

Status: Scholars debate authenticity.

[ Rare Fragment ]

entries.title: Fragment #999 — Unknown
entries.content:
Recovered from the deepest vault beneath the Chronicle

"There was never a chosen one.

There were only people who continued showing up.

Again.

And again.

And again."

— Unknown

The remainder of the fragment has been lost.

[ Legendary Fragment ]

entries.title: Fragment #CH1-001 — The Last Quiet Morning
entries.content:
Recovered from the Early Chronicle

"I remember the morning after the Swarm vanished.

No pings.

No banners.

No red circles demanding attention.

For the first time in years, the Realm was silent.

The silence was unsettling.

Many heroes believed something was wrong.

Several Task Paladins spent hours refreshing things that no longer needed refreshing.

One Code Warlock claimed the silence was suspicious and restarted three perfectly functioning systems.

The Mind Sages simply smiled.

It took several days before the Realm remembered what silence felt like.

Most agreed it was pleasant.

A few admitted they missed the chaos.

The Chronicle records both opinions."

— Unknown Hero

[ Chapter One Reward Fragment ]
